column3:
Loïs‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset comprises geochemical analyses from the Evisa peralkaline rare-metal granite (Corsica
column4: France). A comprehensive analysis of the whole rock and specific mineral species was conducted using multiple analytical methods
column5: including ICP-AES
column6: ICP-MS
column7: EPMA
column8: and LA-ICP-MS. The initial objective of this study was to explore the conditions of crystallization and subsequent alteration of amphibole-bearing rare earth granites
column9: which contain significant concentrations of REE (rare earth elements)
column10: especially the heavier ones (HREE). At Evisa
column11: the distribution of REE within the various rock types was found to be heterogeneous
column12: as evidenced by Whole Rock Analyses
column13: and incorporated into a variety of exotic minerals
column14: the latter being the subject of several types of in-situ analyses. Furthermore
column15:
the numerous generations of hydrothermal alterations highlight the successive remobilization of REE."‡CNRS; Corsica; REE-bearing minerals; peralkalic composition; peralkaline granite; rare-earth‡Dataset‡"EPMA analyses of pyrochlore.tab

The present dataset contains time and space averaged profiles for various turbulent quantities in case of forced and mixed convection in a turbulent pipe flow. The dimensionless numbers for the cases are Re=5300

column10:
0.964).

column11: please check the corresponding paper : High-Fidelity Simulation of Turbulent Mixed Convection in Pipe Flow by Rodrigo Vicente Cruz
column13: Eric Lamballais
column14: Vladimir Duffal
column15:
Erwan Le Coupanec and Sofiane Benhamadouche (submitted to International Journal of Heat and Fluid Flow).

Direct Numerical Simulations are performed to provide a fine description of the turbulent mixed convection flow in an upward heated pipe. The introduction of buoyancy forces in this type of flow gives rise to different non-trivial phenomena such as laminarisation and a bi-stable behaviour
column16: capable of modifying the flow characteristics in a deep and non-monotonic way. The present work aims therefore to establish a high-fidelity and publicly accessible database
column17: containing first and second-order accurate turbulence statistics
column18: as well as budgets of various one-point correlations of turbulent quantities
column19: namely the turbulent kinetic energy and its associated dissipation rate
column20: the temperature variance and its associated dissipation rate
column21: the Reynolds stress tensor and the turbulent heat flux vector. Besides
column22: the coexistence of two and very distinct statistically stationary solutions for a same set of flow parameters is also investigated. These two states are referred to as weak- and strong-turbulence regimes. The budget analyses performed here
column23: such as the original ones presented for the budgets of the Reynolds stresses
column24: contribute to gaining further understanding of the physics of mixed convection flows. The results issued from the present study shall also support the validation of lower-fidelity numerical models

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/1BRHFC‡https://doi.org/10.57745/1BRHFC‡https://doi.org/10.57745/1BRHFC‡espace-generique://10.57745/1BRHFC‡en‡doi:10.57745/1BRHFC‡2023-07-07T13:57:27Z‡2023-07-03T13:00:43Z‡‡Données de réplication pour : Trait-environment associations diverge between native and alien breeding bird assemblages on the world's oceanic islands.‡BARNAGAUD
column3:
Charlotte‡"Lien vers les données
This archive contains the material necessary to replicate the analyses performed in Rault et al (2023) : Trait-environment associations diverge between native and alien breeding bird assemblages on the world's oceanic islands. Global Ecology and Biogeography.

Aim: To investigate spatial variations in the ecological trait structure of breeding bird assemblages across the world’s oceanic islands. To test the hypothesis that native and naturalized alien bird species are filtered by different processes
column4:
leading to diverging associations between traits and insular environmental gradients.

Location: Oceanic islands worldwide.

Time period: Current.

Major taxa studied: Terrestrial breeding  birds.

Methods: We assessed the composition of breeding  terrestrial bird assemblages from the extent-of-occurrence maps of 3170 native and 169 naturalized alien species occurring on 4660 oceanic islands. We quantified the ecological trait structures of species assemblages with respect to diet
column5: mobility
column6: and body mass as the standardized distance between a mean pairwise trait distance index and its expectation from a null model. We used spatial generalized additive models to relate trait structure to proxies of environmental conditions and human impact on land for all species
column7: native species only
column8:
and alien species only. 

Content of the archive: The archive encompasses the data and R script necessary to replicate the analyses described in the Materials and Methods of the associated publication. The data set encompasses data on extant terrestrial breeding birds living on oceanic islands worldwide extracted from Birdlife International's extent of occurrence geodatabase
column9:
and ecological traits associated with these species compiled from multiple sources.
The archive encompasses the data and R script necessary to replicate the analyses described in the Material and Methods of the associated publication. Data collection
column10: compilation
column11:
origin and analytical pipeline are described in the article.

column3:
Richard‡"Lien vers les données
We present here data on crop insurance adoption by farmers in France. Data were collected via an original questionnaire survey available online from February 2021 to October 2021. We collected 465 responses of which 288 were complete.
The survey contained 57 questions divided into 5 parts:
1/ Farm characteristics;
2/ Crop insurance behaviour and preferences;
3/ Determinants of crop insurance adoption;
4/ Expectation for the future;
5/ Socioeconomic characteristics.
We use different methodologies (binary or multiple choices questions
column4: experimental tests
column5: Likert scales
column6: self-ranking
column7: etc.) in order to assess farmers preferences for crop insurance adoption. This data set was used for the first time in the paper: Koenig

David W‡"Lien vers les données
This data set was prepared as a product for the project Perceptions and Valuation of Ecosystem Services in Forests (Perceptions et valorisation des services écosystémiques en forêt) (PERCEVAL)
column5: funded under the national research program Transition in Agriculture
column6: Food Supply and the Environment in Territories (Transition en Territoires de l'Agriculture
column7: l'Alimentation et l'Environnement) (TETRAE) of the French National Institute of Research for Agriculture

et l’Environnement) (INRAE).

Ecosystem services are at the forefront of ecosystem management
column12: and are a featured component of each research themes of the French National Institute of Research for Agriculture
column13: Health
column14: and the Environment (INRAE).  The project Perceptions and Valuation of Ecosystem Services in Forests (PERCEVAL)
column15: funded by INRAE under the national research program Transition in Agriculture
column16: Food Supply and the Environment in Territories
column17: focuses on the measurement and valuation of ecosystem services in the Grand Est region of France
column18:
including the preferences of individuals for forest ecosystem services and potential markets for them (demand) and how ecosystem services are provided in the region (supply).

In this data set
column19: we provide a baseline database of the supply or provisioning of ecosystem services in the Grand Est region of France.  The data estimates a set of twenty-one indicators of seven ecosystem services in the Grand Est
column20: including agriculture production potential
column26: and soil loss by water erosion.  The analysis uses a mix of land use and land cover data
column27: established relationships between ecosystem services and land use/reflectance data
column28: and published maps of ecosystem service supply from the scientific literature. Additionally
column29: we measure the interactions – how a change in one service leads to a change in another – between all ecosystem services considered in the study
column30:
and the contributions of public forests and parks/reserves to the overall provisioning of ecosystem services in the Grand Est.

This data set includes the code and spatial GIS layers needed to construct the final data set of ecosystem service provisioning. Metadata and a step-by-step guide to transforming the spatial data
column31: aligning and resampling the spatial data layers (“rasters”)
column33: and plotting the results are embedded in the code.  The study was conducted using R v.4.5.0
column34: with the ‘terra’ package for the spatial analysis
column35:
and ‘Hmisc’ and ‘corrplot’ packages for calculating and plotting correlation coefficients. (Some preliminary work was conducted in QGIS Desktop v3.34.15 and a Unix shell client. See the documentation embedded in the code for details.)

For more details of the data

This dataset is the result of a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained for the detection of Rooftop Photovoltaic systems. It contains the geographical locations of the Rooftop PhotoVoltaic systems in France as predicted by the model.

This dataset is related to the scientific publication ""Thebault
column38: Ménézo - A comprehensive building-wise Residential Photovoltaic system detection in heterogeneous urban and rural areas: application to French territories"" <i>Applied Energy
column40:
doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2025.125630

It contains the results of applying a CNN classification model to the buildings of each French department. The results are provided as geospatial vector data (in shapefile format: [Shapefile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shapefile)). Among other information described in the Readme file
column41: <b>the 'Score' column/attribute corresponds to the predictive model's output for the corresponding building
column42: representing the likelihood of a PV system being present. The score is a scalar value between 0 (the model is certain that there are no PV system) and 1 (the model is certain that a PV system is present). In the associated publication we consider a threshold of 0.5 (if Score < 0.5
column43: we consider that there is no PV system; if Score > 0.5
column44: we consider that a PV system is present). As discussed in the associated publication
column45:
this threshold value can be adjusted depending on the application of the results.

Important information
1) A large part of the French metropolitan buildings are covered. In a further version all the French departments should be covered
2) Reliability of the results : It is important to refer to the associated publication to understand how reliable are the results. The 'Score' discuss above provide a quantification on the model's confidence in his classification. Additionnaly
column46:

column3:
Etienne‡"Lien vers les données
Glacier ice flow velocity is an important variable to document the past and current status of the glacier worldwide. Our aim with this dataset is to provide innovative products for glaciers and their environments from remote sensing data for some mountain ranges in the Tropical Andes in southern Peru and Bolivia. The data set proposed here includes maps of annual glacier surface flow velocities for the period 2013-2022
column4: created from Sentinel-2 optical data with the work-flow presented in Mouginot et al. (2023) and Rabatel et al. (2023). It can be used for the monitoring of glacier dynamics or for hazards detection associated to glaciers destabilization
column5: as well as an input of model calibration and validation.  These products are distributed in both netCDF and GeoTiff formats
column6: georeferenced under the UTM-19S projection and with a pixel resolution of 50 metres. In the netCDF the following variables can be found: - v(time
column7: y
column8: x): Annual ice surface velocity between 2013-2022 in metres per year - dir(y
column9: x): Direction of the ice flow represented as the angle between the flow vector and x-axis. vx = v*cos(direction) and vy = v*sin(direction) - cnt(y
column10: x): Number of measurements used for the regression - stdev(y
column11: x): The error on surface ice speed  (metres per year) is given as the standard deviation obtained from the results of the regression. - stdeva(y
column12: x): The error on surface ice speed (metres per year) direction is given as the standard deviation obtained from the results of the regression. - flag(y
column13: x): Error flag mask on pixels considered as unreliable. Pixels with a coefficient of variation of the glacier surface flow velocity higher than 75% and with a standard deviation of the glacier flow direction higher than 2.5° are masked. Masked pixels: 0 Non-masked pixels: NaN - trend(y
column14: x): Trends generated from velocities in metres/year**2
column15: with a median filter of size 5 pixels. -trend_mask(y
column17: based on Kendall rank of correlation.   The same layers can be found as separated geotiff files

This dataset contains images of the rooftops of French buildings
column36: with a large portion of the images from metropolitan France available. Ultimately

This dataset is related to the scientific publication ""Thebault
column43: Ménézo - A comprehensive building-wise Residential Photovoltaic system detection in heterogeneous urban and rural areas: application to French territories"" <i>Applied Energy
column45:
doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2025.125630

Aerial Land Imagery
The aerial imagery used in this study comes from the Institut National de l'Information Géographique et Forestière (IGN)
column46: the French national geographic institute. These images are provided in 25 km² RGB tiles with a resolution of 20 cm. The tiles are organized by French department and are freely accessible as JP2 raster files BD Ortho - Institut National Géographique. All the imagery utilized in this project is less than four years old. The availability of department-level imagery is fundamental to our methodology
column47:
as both cadastral data processing and the analysis of CNN model predictions are performed at this geographic scale.

Building Registry
The location and geometry of each building were extracted from a national building registry distributed by the French Etalab project. For each French department
column48: a single SHP file is provided
column49:
containing building geometries stored as polygon features.

Data Post-Processing
Both raster (aerial imagery) and vector (building registry) data were processed using PyQGIS via QGIS. The preprocessing of vector data follows several steps. First
column50: polygons with an area smaller than 10 m² were discarded
column53: which are unlikely to host PV panels. Next
column54: a 4-meter buffer was applied to each polygon to account for the frequent spatial discrepancies between the building registry and the actual building locations. To include additional contextual information in each final image and accommodate these shifts
column55: each polygon was replaced with its oriented rectangular bounding box
column56: minimizing the area of the box. Finally
column57:
the X and Y coordinates and a department-based unique identifier were added to each polygon feature.

Creation of Building Images
Each building polygon was intersected with the corresponding aerial imagery raster to generate a cropped image. These images were saved to individual files. For example
column59: one of the more populated regions of France
column62: with an average image size of 120x120 pixels. Notably
column63: 97.9% of these images are smaller than 250x250 pixels. Approximately 1.5% of buildings span multiple raster tiles
column64:
resulting in final images that do not fully capture the entire rooftop.

column3:
André‡"Lien vers les données
Monetary emission factors to estimate the carbon footprint associated to purchases in research laboratories. Emissions embodied in goods and services can be estimated by measuring physical or monetary flows. Physical estimates are more accurate if emission factors and inventories for each product exist. This is not the case for the sheer amount of goods and services purchased in research laboratories. To make the problem tractable
column4: goods were classified according to the French system for accountability in research (NACRES)
column5: to which we manually associated monetary emission factors (EFs) in kg CO2e/euro and for which monetary inventories are readily available through the laboratory accountability. All euro values correspond to year 2019. The emissions of good i can calculated as e(i) = p(i)* EF(i)
column6: with p(i) its tax-free price in euro. EFs were estimated using the three approaches sketched in Fig. 1 of the associated publication: i) an environmentally extended input-output (EEIO) method that we will call in the following macro and note the resulting EFs EFmacro; ii) a process-based method that we  call micro EFmicro (or LCA for life cycle assessment); and iii) an intermediate approach based on the carbon footprint of selected companies of the research sector
column7:
that we call meso. column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/XMPAVS‡https://doi.org/10.57745/XMPAVS‡https://doi.org/10.57745/XMPAVS‡espace-generique://10.57745/XMPAVS‡‡doi:10.57745/XMPAVS‡2023-12-01T15:30:05Z‡2022-07-27T12:20:53Z‡‡Data obtained during a field campaign conducted in spring 2018 within the framework of AgriMultiPol program

column3: Julien‡KAMMER
column4:
Julien‡"Lien vers les données
The data in this repository corresponds to data used in the article New Particle Formation at a mixed peri-urban and agricultural site (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.159370). The context of data acquisition and their interpretation are given by the following abstract :
New Particle Formation (NPF) is a major source of ultrafine particles that affect both air quality and climate. Despite emissions from agricultural activities having a strong potential to lead to NPF
column5: little is known about NPF within agricultural environments. The aim of the present study was to investigate the occurrence of NPF events at an agricultural site
column6: and any potential relationship between agricultural emissions and NPF events. A field campaign was conducted for 3 months at the FR-Gri-ICOS site (France)
column7:
at an experimental farm 25 km west of Paris city centre.

16 NPF events have been identified from the analysis of particle number size distributions; 8 during the daytime
column8: and 8 during the night-time. High solar radiation and ozone mixing ratios were observed during the days NPF occurred
column9: suggesting photochemistry plays a key role in daytime NPF. These events were also associated with higher levels of VOCs such as isoprene
column10: methanol
column11: or toluene compared to non-event days. However
column12: ammonia levels were lower during daytime NPF events
column13:
contributing to the hypothesis that daytime NPF events were not related to agricultural activities.

On the other hand
column14: temperature and ozone were lower during the nights when NPF events were observed
column15: whereas relative humidity was higher. During these nights
column16: higher concentrations of NO2 and ammonia were observed. As a result
column18: in particular the spreading of fertiliser on surrounding crops
column19:
are suspected to contribute to night-time NPF events.

Finally
column20: all the identified NPF events were also observed at SIRTA monitoring station 20 km from the FR-Gri ICOS site
column21: showing that both night-time and daytime NPF events were regional processes. We hypothesise that night-time NPF may be related to fertiliser spreading over a regional scale
column22: as opposed to the local activities at the farm. To our knowledge
column23:
this is the first time night-time NPF has been observed in the agricultural context.

column3:
Emmanuel‡"Lien vers les données
Resveratrol and its derivatives
column4: such as dimers
column5: are of great interest for pharmaceutic and cosmetic applications. Nevertheless
column6: bioproduction of dimers of resveratrol suffers from low yield – due to the competition of different radical-radical coupling patterns - and complex isolation procedures
column7: whereas organic synthetic pathways remain long
column8: fastidious without affording higher yield. Although enzymatic routes allow to synthesize dimers in one step from resveratrol
column9: biocatalysis optimisation is required to improve yield and oriented radical-radical coupling selectivity toward a specific resveratrol dimer
column10: E-Labruscol herein. Labruscol have demonstrated anticarcinogenic properties against two human skin malignant melanoma cancer cell lines. More investigations are required to decipher all its potential. Labruscol
column11: was isolated from V. Labrusca L. cell suspensions culture. However
column12: as 10 L of medium was required to recovered 41 mg of Labruscol
column13: a new approach had to be considered to increase this yield. Although δ-viniferin was often reported as the major product present in the relatively complex mixture of oligomers obtained via the enzymatic dimerization of resveratrol
column14: Ponzoni et al. have reported the presence of very small amount of E-Labruscol (≈5%) during laccase-mediated dimerization of resveratrol. Building on these findings
column15: and having successfully achieved the quite challenging enzymatic synthesis of syringaresinol from sinapyl alcohol by reversing the initial selectivity of the enzyme for the β-O-4 dimer in a previous work
column16: the aim of this study was to increase the yield of E-Labruscol by fine-tuning the biocatalysis conditions.  After a rapid screening of conditions
column17: the Design of Experiments (DoE) was implemented to access E-Labruscol through a laccase-mediated dimerization of resveratrol. Selected parameters – i.e.
column18: concentration in resveratrol
column20: composition of the medium
column21: addition rate of the enzymatic solution - were tested and included in the DoE to find the best conditions to yield E-Labruscol in large amounts. Two diastereomers of E-Labruscol
column23: were identified and isolated as major products (25% and 52% yields
column24: respectively). Recovery of the target compounds from enzymatic solution was achieved through centrifugal partition chromatography (CPC)
column25: a technic that has already been proven efficient for the separation of grapevine cells culture and avoids the use of waste-generating silica gel chromatography.  After CPC
column26: fractions were submitted to 13C NMR dereplication workflow to identify compounds. When new structures were detected
column27: preparative liquid chromatography was performed to isolate the targeted compounds. Then
column28: structure elucidation of these compounds was achieved through NMR analysis (1D and 2D) and HRMS. This biocatalytic process was then successfully scaled-up (starting with 2 g of resveratrol) and provided 410 mg of E-Labruscol. This optimized laccase-mediated synthesis of E-Labruscol from resveratrol allows to
column29: not only envisage subsequent (bio)chemical transformations of Labruscol to access novel compounds of interest
column30: but also carry out biological activities assays. This dataset relates with the optimisation of an enzymatic-mediated oxidation of resveratrol toward Labruscol. It contains data about the activity of the enzyme
column31:
the design of experimented that has been performed and structural elucidation of new isolated dimers of resveratrol."‡CNRS; Green chemistry; Resveratrol; chromatography; labruscol; stilbenes‡Dataset‡"DoE_Glycostil_Labruscol.zip

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/BFEXCE‡https://doi.org/10.57745/BFEXCE‡https://doi.org/10.57745/BFEXCE‡espace-generique://10.57745/BFEXCE‡en‡doi:10.57745/BFEXCE‡2023-07-31T12:53:37Z‡2023-07-04T06:53:58Z‡‡Données de réplication pour : Trait-independent habitat associations explain low co-occurrence in native and exotic birds on a tropical volcanic island‡BARNAGAUD
column3:
Jean-Yves‡"Lien vers les données
On oceanic islands
column4: ecosystem processes hold on a limited number of species. However
column5: their patterns of coexistence and the resulting variations of assemblage composition along environmental gradients often remain insufficiently known to set up efficient conservation measures in the face of increasing anthropogenic pressure. In particular
column6: stronger human impacts on insular as opposed to continental habitats
column7: as well as the massive introduction of exotic species on islands
column8: modify the functional composition of species assemblages and may alter key ecological functions. Here
column9: we explored the distributions of terrestrial bird species
column10: and the resulting taxonomic and functional compositions of their assemblages
column11: along the main habitat gradients structuring landscapes in La Réunion
column12: an oceanic island located in the Madagascan region. We used multivariate analyses and generalized additive models to analyze a set of 372 point counts
column13: surveyed 10 years before the establishment of La Réunion National Park. So doing
column14: we established a reference point for the impacts of exotic species and human alterations of habitats on the island’s bird assemblages prior to the setting of an ecosystem-level conservation strategy. We found that native and exotic species overlapped at mid-elevations
column15: but the former were tied to native habitats while the latter were associated with urban areas and man-modified landscape mosaics. Species with contrasting trophic preferences were segregated along habitat gradients. However
column17: ecological traits had a weak role in explaining variations in the composition of bird assemblages. We conclude that native and exotic species in La Réunion formed two distinct species assemblages both in terms of traits and habitat preferences
column18: therefore unlikely to interact over available resources. However
column19: several ongoing processes including recent introductions
column20: continued anthropogenic pressures on habitats
column21:
and the conservation measures related to La Réunion National Park may all have recently altered these patterns. Performing further analyses of historical datasets complemented with diachronic surveys therefore appears essential to monitor the efficiency of recently-established conservation measures in favour of insular ecosystems.

This archive contains all the data and R scripts necessary to replicate the analyses performed on this article.

The dataset was obtained with four RNA extraction protocols applied on 5 mL of raw wastewater (influent) samples. The protocols used were : - Protocol Z = commercial kit
column4: based on silica column
column5: and specific to wastewater samples. - Protocol NS1 = commercial kit
column6: based on silica beads
column7: and non specific to wastewater samples. - Protocol NS2 = Protocol NS1 with a neutral phenol chloroform treatment applied just after the lysis step. - Protocol SB = homemade protocol
column9: developed fo wastewater samples. Two kinds of analyses were performed on the samples: the quantification of the SARS-CoV-2 genome by targeting the E gene
column10: the detection of RT-qPCR inhibitors by the quantification of F-specific RNA phages of genogroup II (FRNAPH-II). The delta Cq value between undiluted and diluted samples was used to detect the presence of RT-qPCR inhibitors. The analyses were carried out in three laboratories
column11: with each comparing the Z protocol to one of the three other protocols (NS1
column12: NS2 or SB). Each lab analysed influent samples collected in three wastewater treatment plant (WWTPs) with three distinct samples per WWTPs (n=9 per laboratory). Each sample was analyzed five times
column13: giving 45 data points per laboratory. The dataset allows the comparison of the RNA extraction protocols for both RNA concentrations (SARS-CoV-2) and removal of RT-qPCR inhibitors (FRNAPH-II)

‡Physics-based Simulations of 3D Wave Propagation with Source Variability: HEMEW^S-3D‡Gatti
column6: Filippo‡Lehmann
column7:
Fanny‡"Lien vers les données
The HEMEW^S-3D database contains 30
column8: 000 simulation results of the 3D elastic wave equation. This equation governs the propagation of waves in a 3D propagation medium. Elastic waves originate from a deep source with random location and random orientation. Three types of data are given in this database: a materials dataset
column9: source properties
column10: and a velocity dataset.  The major difference between the HEMEW^S-3D database and the previous version HEMEW-3D is the source. It was fixed in the first version while in the current version
column11:
both its position and orientation vary. Each material describes the propagation domain used for one numerical simulation. It is built from non-stationary random fields and corresponds to the velocity of shear waves. All materials are 3D arrays of shape 32 x 32 x 32. A different source is used for each simulation. It is described by its coordinates and the three angles that parametrize its moment tensor. The velocity dataset contains the velocity wavefields simulated at the surface of each propagation domain. Each velocity field has 3 spatial components and 800 time steps (between 0 and 8s). A readme file gives more details on the data and can be found below (search for ""readme"" in the search bar"")"‡CNRS; Machine Learning; artificial intelligence; dataset; deep learning; differential equation; digital simulation; kinematic wave; simulation model; three dimensional digital model; time series‡Dataset‡"material100000-101999.npy

Antoine‡"Lien vers les données
Glacier ice flow velocity is an important variable to document the past and current status of the glacier worldwide. The aim of the ESA AlpGlacier project is to create innovative products for glaciers and their environments from remote sensing data for the European Alps mountain range. The data set proposed here includes maps of annual glacier surface flow velocities for the period 2015-2021
column4: created from Sentinel-2 optical data with the work-flow presented in Mouginot et al.
column5: 2023. It can be used for the monitoring of glacier dynamics or for hazards detection associated to glaciers destabilization
column6: as well as an input of models calibration and validation. These products are distributed in both netCDF and GeoTiff formats
column7: georeferenced under the UTM-32N projection."‡CNRS; ENV environment and natural resources; Glacier; Glacier surface flow velocity; Passive Remote Sensing; S2A

column3: on carabid communities and weed seed predation in winter cereals‡CARBONNE
column4: Benjamin‡CARBONNE
column5:
Benjamin‡"Lien vers les données
This data repository is associated with the article ‘Cascading effects of landscape
column6: mediated by mesoclimate
column7: on carabid communities and weed seed predation in winter cereals’. It includes both the datasets used (climatic
column9: agricultural practices and biological data). Below is the abstract of the article:   Agricultural intensification
column10: landscape simplification
column11: and climate change threaten biodiversity and ecosystem services in arable lands. Increasing semi-natural habitats and landscape heterogeneity can mitigate these impacts by providing diverse habitats
column13: and modifying mesoclimate. As effective natural enemies in arable lands
column14: carabids play a key role in pest and weed seed regulation and are influenced by field management and landscape. This study hypothesized that field management and landscape factors directly
column15: as well as indirectly (via mesoscale air temperature)
column16: influence carabid communities and weed seed predation. We sampled 77 winter cereal fields across 20 landscape windows selected along uncorrelated gradients of semi-natural habitat amount and landscape heterogeneity. We monitored air temperature
column18: and weed seed predation during two sampling periods in late spring and early summer 2023. Piecewise Structural Equation Models were built to test for the direct and indirect effects of local factors (farming practices and standing weeds)
column19: landscape and mesoclimate on carabids and weed seed predation. Results showed that the amount semi-natural habitats and landscape heterogeneity primarily influence carabid activity density and composition
column20: which in turn affect weed seed predation. Grasslands
column22: favour carabids but also increase maximum air temperature
column24: likely due to thermotolerance traits. In addition
column25: a cascading effect of farming practices on seed predation was also observed in late spring
column26: mediated by carabid activity-density. The study highlights the importance of semi-natural habitats
column28: and farming practices in shaping carabid communities and their ecosystem services in arable fields. We highlight the influence of the landscape components on mesoscale air temperatures
column29:
impacting the species composition of carabid communities and potentially affecting weed regulation services through seed predation."‡INRAE; CNRS; ECOBIO; IGEPP‡Dataset; Other‡"ASCC.txt

column3:
Edouard‡"Lien vers les données
This study models the evolution of the French electric vehicles fleet depending on different parameters
column4: such as production or recycling capacities
column5: average lithium amount per vehicle
column6: using system dynamics. Following the European Union's 2023 decision to ban internal combustion engine vehicles by 2035
column7: France has focused on securing critical raw materials
column9: to support its electrification goals. The system dynamics model incorporates the target growth of the French electric vehicles fleet
column10: lithium extraction from a newly established mine
column11:
and recycling rates mandated by European regulations. The results show that reaching the 2050 target is not the only issue: maintaining the fleet at a stable state is not evident if the three parameters mentioned above are not balanced. This study concludes that the European Union’s legal targets are not enough to ensure the perennity of the electric vehicles fleet."‡CNRS; Critical raw materials; Electric vehicles; Energy transition; Extraction; Lithium supply; Mobility; Recycling; System dynamics‡Dataset; Image; Model‡"Data.tab

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/CPS1V3‡https://doi.org/10.57745/CPS1V3‡https://doi.org/10.57745/CPS1V3‡inrae://10.57745/CPS1V3‡en‡doi:10.57745/CPS1V3‡2025-10-07T18:01:24Z‡2025-10-07T18:00:55Z‡‡Endozoochorous plant dispersal by brown bear (Ursus arctos) in the Pyrénées: angiosperms but also ferns and mosses and the importance of disaggregation agents‡Pauly
column3:
Grégoire‡"Lien vers les données
Endozoochory by megafauna enables temperate mountain plants to disperse over long distance. As an endozoochorous agent
column4: brown bear may play a unique role through its biology and ecology especially because of its very diverse diet (angiosperms
column5: ferns
column6: mosses). However
column7: few studies have been conducted on the dispersal of all angiosperms and none on ferns or mosses. Also
column8: the fact that the faecal matrix could represent a barrier for the germination of dispersed seeds due to various constraints
column9:
has often been neglected.

In this study
column10: we investigated endozoochory by the European brown bear and the effect of the faecal matrix on the germination success. We conducted a seedling emergence monitoring experiment in greenhouse during seven months to assess seed dispersal potential based on 86 faeces. Each sample was split into two modalities (raw vs. filtered faeces)
column11:
to test the effect of the faecal matrix on seedling germination and growth.

We found that brown bear dispersed not only fleshy-fruited plants
column13: and for the first time among Ursids
column14:
ferns and mosses. We also pointed out that the faecal matrix acts as an important barrier for germination with little effect on growth.

These results highlight the role of Brown Bear in the dispersal of non-fleshy-fruited plants
column15: and suggest that the spectrum of plant species dispersed by Brown Bear is wider than formerly thought. They also stress the importance of faeces disaggregation on seed dispersal outcomes
column16:
a mechanism mediated by abiotic or biotic agents."‡CNRS; Dispersal effectiveness; Ecosystem engineers; Faecal matrix; Fern; Long distance dispersal; Megafauna; Moss; Plant dispersal; Seed dispersal; Ursus arctos; dispersal efffectiveness; feacal matrix‡Dataset‡"Data_base_1_Vascular_plant_dispersal.csv

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/NW0GF9‡https://doi.org/10.57745/NW0GF9‡https://doi.org/10.57745/NW0GF9‡univ-lorraine://10.57745/NW0GF9‡en‡doi:10.57745/NW0GF9‡2024-05-23T11:48:10Z‡2024-05-21T09:00:08Z‡‡Supporting information for: Behavior and speciation of lanthanides during standardized ecotoxicity tests with Daphnia magna: implications and recommendations for hazard and risk assessment.‡Vignati
column3:
Davide‡"Lien vers les données
These files are the supporting information for the article: ""Behavior and speciation of lanthanides during standardized ecotoxicity tests with Daphnia magna: implications and recommendations for hazard and risk assessment"". The dataset contains sixteen tables (Table S1 to Table S16) and four figures (Figure S1 to Figure S4). The supplementary tables and figures are numbered according to their first mention in the text of the corresponding article. Tables S1 and S2 provide information on the chemicals used in the study and on the composition of the test medium used to expose the specimen of Daphnia magna to the lanthanides
column4: respectively.   Details about the exposure concentrations used in the experiments are given in Table S3
column5: while Table S4 summarizes the raw results (number of surviving organisms after 24 and 48 hours of exposure) for all ecotoxicological experiments. Table S5 contains the figures of merit for analytical measurement of lanthanides concentrations by ICP-MS. The corresponding raw analytical results for all measurements are reported in Table S9.  Table S6 reports selected values of solubility products for the carbonate species of lanthanides that can form in the ecotoxicological test medium (see Table S2) used in the present study. These values have been used to estimate the theoretical solubility limits of lanthanides in the same medium (Table S12).  Table S7 and S8 contain measured pH values for solutions amended with different concentrations of lanthanides and the proposed linear relationships between lanthanides concentrations and pH values
column6: respectively.  Table S10 reports the values and 95% confidence intervals for the effect concentrations causing the death of 10% (EC10) or 50% (EC50) of the exposed organism after 24 and 48 hours of exposure to lanthanides. These values are based on nominal concentrations and are compared with analytically measured average exposure concentrations in Table S11.  Table S13 compares the potential contribution of counterions 'chloride and nitrate' to the observed ecotoxicity in daphnids exposed to lanthanides salts (see table S1).  Table S14 contains methematical linear relationships between selected EC50 values and the atomic mass of each lanthanides. The predictions obtained form these relationships and the experimental values are compared in Table S15.  Table S16 provides a detailed (albeit not exhaustive) list of caveats
column7: recommendations and open issues related to the ecotoxicity of lanthanides to aquatic organisms. Captions for all tables and figures are contained in the readme file of the database along with a detailed description of all variables."‡CNRS; ecotoxicology

A high-fidelity simulation of buoyancy-assisted mixed convection in a turbulent pipe flow is performed to investigate the spatial transition from the forced to the mixed convection regime. The configuration is representative of pressurised water in industrial applications (10^7 Pa and 400 K)
column4: with a Reynolds number
column5: a Prandtl number and a Richardson number of 7483
column7: respectively
column9:
the mass flow rate and the imposed heat flux. The thermal entry length is relatively long: skin friction and Nusselt number reach fully developed values after 70 diameters. The development zone shows a complex behaviour with a partial relaminarisation and a turbulence redevelopment associated with strong temperature fluctuations. These phenomena can be observed for the first time thanks to the use of a very long computational domain based on inflow/outflow boundary conditions with a realistic turbulence injection at the inlet."‡CNRS; buoyancy; fluid dynamics; forced convection; turbulence; turbulent pipe flow‡Dataset‡"plot.py

column3:
Quentin‡"Lien vers les données
Le jeu de données a été réalisé dans le cadre du projet de thèse
column4: qui vise à mieux appréhender les caractéristiques géologiques des calcaires du Jurassique moyen et de l’Oxfordien du Bassin Nord-Aquitain. Pour répondre aux problématiques scientifiques spécifiques
column5: de nombreuses données sédimentologiques
column6: diagénétiques
column7: géochimiques et géochronologiques ont été acquises. Les données de la partie (« sédimentologie ») de la thèse permettent de quantifier l’influence couplée de l’accommodation et des fluctuations climatiques à long terme sur la production carbonatée et les types de producteurs carbonatés
column8: que ce soit à l’échelle du Bassin Aquitain mais également sur l’ouest de la France. Les données sont issues d’affleurements et de forages. Les taux d’accommodation (m/Ma) et taux d’accommodation affranchie de l’isostasie (m/Ma) ont été calculés pour 5 périodes géologiques dans 37 localités dans le Bassin Nord-Aquitain et dans 70 localités dans l’ouest du Bassin de Paris (« Annexe1_chapitres 4 et 5_accommodation »). Les taux d’accumulation de carbonates (m/Ma) sont calculés dans les mêmes localités et pour les mêmes périodes géologiques (« Annexe2_chapitres 4 et 5_accumulation de carbonates »). Un type de producteur carbonaté dominant est associé pour ces 5 intervalles stratigraphiques sur chacune des localités du Bassin Nord-Aquitain et de l’ouest du Bassin de Paris (« Annexe3_chapitres 4 et 5_producteurs carbonatés »). Les données acquises dans la partie (« diagenèse des carbonates ») de la thèse permettent de reconstruire l’histoire diagénétique et paléohydrologique de la bordure nord-est du Bassin Aquitain et de proposer une méthodologie pour dater les processus diagénétiques (néomorphisme
column9: dolomitisation et dissolution/karstification) et in fine pour comprendre leur influence sur la distribution des propriétés réservoirs. Ces données ont été acquises sur des ciments carbonatés (calcites et dolomites). Les compositions en éléments majeurs (Ca
column10: Mg
column11: Fe
column13: Al et Sr) ont été mesurées à l'aide de la microsonde électronique SX-Five (« Annexe4_chapitre6_données microsonde électronique »). Les données isotopiques en δ13C et δ18O ont été acquises par deux méthodes : (1) par spectrométrie de masse à rapports isotopiques (« isotope-ratio mass spectrometry »
column14: IRMS) et (2) par spectrométrie de masse des ions secondaires (« secondary ion mass spectrometry »
column15: SIMS). Des mesures en δ18O et δ13C ont également été effectuées à partir de l'analyse des « clumped isotopes » (IRMS Δ47). Toutes les données des isotopes stables de l’oxygène et du carbone sont rassemblées dans le fichier « Annexe5_chapitre 6_données IRMS et SIMS ». Les compositions en éléments majeurs
column16: mineurs et traces et la composition en éléments des terres rares ont été mesurées à l'aide d’un spectromètre de masse à plasma à couplage inductif haute résolution Element XR de Thermo ScientificTM couplé à un système d’ablation laser (LA-HR-ICP-MS) et synthétisées dans le fichier « Annexe 6_chapitre 6_majeurs
column18: traces et ETR ». Les données utilisées pour caractériser l’évolution de l’histoire d’enfouissement et d’exhumation de la bordure nord-est du Bassin Aquitain
column19: modélisée à partir du logiciel Petromod 1D
column20: ont été regroupées dans le fichier « Annexe7_chapitre 6_Petromod1D ». Le traitement des données issues de la méthode de datation U-Pb sur calcites à partir d’un LA-HR-ICP-MS est effectué à partir du fichier « Annexe8_chapitres 6 et 7_traitement données U-Pb ». Enfin
column21: la description détaillée de soixante-sept affleurements (« Annexe9_coupes sédimentaires ») permet de déterminer la lithologie
column24: les structures sédimentaires et biogéniques.   The dataset was produced as part of a thesis project in order to better understanding the geological characteristics of the Middle Jurassic and Oxfordian limestones of the northern Aquitaine Basin. Consequently
column28: geochemical and geochronological data was acquired. The data in the ""sedimentology” part of the thesis enable us to quantify the coupled influence of accommodation and long-term eccentricity variations on carbonate accumulation rates for numerous carbonate factories during Middle Jurassic to Oxfordian
column29: both at the scale of the Aquitaine Basin and in western France. Data were obtained from outcrops and boreholes. Accommodation rates (m/Ma) and non-isostatic accommodation rates (m/Ma) were calculated for 5 geological periods in 37 localities in the northern Aquitain Basin and in 70 localities in the western Paris Basin (""Annexe1_chapitres 4 et 5_accommodation""). The carbonate accumulation rates (m/Ma) are calculated in the same localities and for the same geological periods (""Annexe2_chapitres 4 et 5_accumulation de carbonates""). A dominant type of carbonate producer is associated with each of the localities in the northern Aquitaine Basin and western Paris Basin for these 5 stratigraphic intervals (""Annexe3_chapitres 4 et 5_producteurs carbonatés""). The data acquired in the ""carbonate diagenesis"" part of the thesis enable us to reconstruct the diagenetic and paleohydrological history of the northeastern Aquitaine Basin and to propose a newly innovative methodology for dating diagenetic processes (neomorphism
column30: dolomitization and dissolution/karstification) and ultimately for understanding their influence on the distribution of reservoir properties. These data were acquired on carbonate cements (calcites and dolomites). The major element compositions (Ca
column34: Al and Sr) were measured using the SX-Five electron microprobe (""Annexe4_chapitre6_données microsonde électronique""). Isotopic data in δ13C and δ18O were acquired by two methods: (1) isotope-ratio mass spectrometry (IRMS) and (2) secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS). Measurements in δ18O and δ13C were also made using clumped isotope analysis (IRMS Δ47). The oxygen and carbon isotopic data are compiled in the file ""Annexe5_chapitre 6_données IRMS et SIMS"". Major
column36: trace and Rare Earth Element compositions were measured using a laser ablation coupled to a sector field inductively plasma mass spectrometer from Thermo Scientific (LA-HR-ICP-MS) and summarized in the file ""Annexe 6_chapitre 6_majeurs
column38: traces et ETR"". The data used to characterize the evolution of the burial and exhumation history of the northeastern Aquitaine Basin
column40: have been compiled in the file ""Annexe7_chapitre 6_Petromod1D"". Data from the LA-HR-ICP-MS method of U-Pb dating on calcites are processed in the file ""Annexe8_chapitre 6 et 7_traitement données U-Pb”. Finally
column41: the detailed description of sixty-seven outcrop sections (« Annexe9_coupes sédimentaires ») provides information on lithology

sedimentary and biogenic structures."‡CNRS; Diagenèse; Géochimie isotopique; Géochronologie; Pétrographie; Sédimentologie‡Dataset‡"Annexe1_chapitres 4 et 5_accommodation.tab

The data analyzed in this study pertains to Ferralsols sampled from the Brazilian Central Plateau. These soils are located within two distinct geomorphological regions: the Late Tertiary South American Surface and the Late Quaternary Velhas Surface
column4:
each characterized by specific topographical and soil properties.

Geomorphological and Soil Context
1. Late Tertiary South American Surface: This surface corresponds to tablelands with altitudes generally between 900 and 1200 meters. The dominant soil type in this region is gibbsitic-sesquioxidic Ferralsols.
2. Late Quaternary Velhas Surface: Positioned 5 to 25 meters below the South American Surface
column5:
this area features a moderate slope and is predominantly covered by kaolinitic-non-sesquioxidic Ferralsols.

Soil Types and Classification
The Ferralsols under investigation are classified based on their morphological and mineralogical properties according to the World Reference Base for Soil Resources (WRB). These include:

column3:
Jérôme‡"Lien vers les données
Jeu de données issue de l'étude xKy.
Le projet xky a deux objectifs principaux. Le premier est de fournir des passerelles se branchant sur les compteurs Linky afin de permettre aux personnes désireuses de comprendre et de réduire leur dépense énergétique de pouvoir suivre en temps réel leur consommation électrique.
Le second est de pouvoir permettre de développer un lien entre les citoyens et le monde de la recherche au travers d'une expérience de science participative dans le domaine des énergies électriques grâce à la transparence de ses passerelles dont les plans et codes sont mis à disposition en OpenSource sous licence CC-BY-NC-ND.
Si vous trouvez ce jeu de données utile ou vous avez des questions
column4:
n'hésitez pas à écrire dans le topic sur le forum OTE.

Le jeu de données contient actuellement les données de:
Une partie individualisée qui couvre la période d'octobre 2023 à Mars 2024 avec
- Les données données socio-techniques des 255 participants à l'étude
- Les données brutes individuelles anonymisées issues des linky de 153 participants à l'étude (Indexs

column6: Tarification en cours

- Les données recalculées à partir des données brutes 153 participants à l'étude (Consommation journalière
column9: Puissance active
column10:
...) avec pas d'échantillonnage inférieurs à la minute
- Les données météo attachées au particpant sont issues du site https://open-meteo.com/ et sont à l'échelle départementale.
Une partie Globale qui couvre la période d'octobre 2023 à Mars 2024 avec:
- Consommation jour moyenne des participants de l'étude ainsi que par type de contrat
- Puissance apparente moyenne des participants de l'étude ainsi que par type de contrat
- Puissance active moyenne des participants de l'étude ainsi que par type de contrat
- Facteur de puissance moyenne des participants de l'étude ainsi que par type de contrat
